lessonTitle: Definition of Done — The Quality Gate
learningObjectives: Create a team Definition of Done with quality standards that apply to every user story
Distinguish between Definition of Done (universal quality) and Acceptance Criteria (story-specific)
Understand how DoD ensures incrementally releasable product increments
Evolve DoD as team capability grows — start basic, add as tools and skills mature
Prevent the "90% done" syndrome where features work but aren't production-ready

content.headline: Five Stories Done — Or Are They? Morgan Says Yes, Maya Says No Way.
content.intro: You're **Alex Mitchell**, Scrum Master at Savory & Co. Sprint 1 just wrapped. Morgan proudly reports all 5 stories are done — every acceptance criterion passed. But Maya raises a red flag: code isn't reviewed, test coverage is only 40%, and nothing is deployed to staging. Carlos is confused: "Can we launch or not?" The team has two different definitions of "done," and without a shared quality gate, nobody knows the truth.

failure_cards.title: The Launch That Crashed
failure_cards.summary: Features pass acceptance criteria but crash in production
failure_cards.details: Morgan declared all 5 stories done — filters worked, search was fast, sorting was accurate. Carlos approved a customer launch. Within hours, the app crashed: untested edge cases broke the search, unreviewed code had a memory leak, and nothing had been tested in a staging environment. Maya: "It worked on my machine — but it was never deployed anywhere real." The rollback cost 3 days of emergency fixes. All because "done" meant "it works locally" instead of "it's production-ready."

failure_cards.title: The 90% Done Sprint
failure_cards.summary: Team counts incomplete stories as "done," inflating velocity
failure_cards.details: Sprint after sprint, Morgan reported 15-20 story points completed. But Maya tracked the reality: half the stories had no code reviews, 40% test coverage, and zero staging deployments. When Carlos asked "why does nothing feel shippable?" Alex discovered the team was counting AC-met-but-not-quality-checked stories as done. Real velocity was half what they reported. Planning became unreliable because it was based on inflated numbers. Carlos: "I'd rather know we completed 8 real stories than pretend we completed 16."

failure_cards.title: The Technical Debt Avalanche
failure_cards.summary: Skipping code reviews and tests creates compounding technical debt
failure_cards.details: Without a DoD requiring code review and test coverage, the team shipped fast for 4 sprints. By Sprint 5, every new feature broke an old one. Maya spent 60% of each sprint fixing regressions instead of building new features. Devon: "I'm afraid to touch anything — I don't know what my change will break." The codebase became fragile and unmaintainable. Alex realized: "We saved 2 hours per story by skipping reviews. Now we're spending 8 hours per story on bug fixes."

character_quote.name: Maya Rodriguez
character_quote.quote: Done means done-done, not 'it works on my machine.' Without a Definition of Done, every developer has a different quality bar — and the lowest bar wins.

content.learning_hook: The **Definition of Done** is a shared team agreement specifying quality standards that EVERY user story must meet. It's the quality gate that separates "features work" from "product is shippable." Let's learn how to create, apply, and evolve a DoD.

interaction.title: Creating and Applying the Quality Gate
interaction.description: Morgan thinks her stories are done. Maya disagrees. Alex facilitates a DoD workshop to create a shared quality gate — and then the team must apply it to real sprint work.

questions.question: Which items belong in Definition of Done? Select all that apply.
questions.situation: Alex facilitates a Sprint 0 DoD workshop. The team proposes items for their Definition of Done. Some items are universal quality standards; others vary by story and don't belong in DoD.

options.text: Code reviewed by at least one peer developer
Unit tests with 80% or greater code coverage
Story delivers at least $10K business value
options.whyWrong: Business value varies dramatically per story. A critical bug fix and a major feature have different value propositions. DoD must apply equally to ALL stories.

options.text: Deployed to staging environment
Customer feedback collected for this feature
options.whyWrong: Customer feedback requires user access and scheduling that isn't feasible for every story in every sprint. It's valuable but not a universal quality gate.

options.text: Acceptance criteria verified by Product Owner

questions.explanation: DoD items must apply universally to EVERY story. Code review, test coverage, staging deployment, and AC verification are quality standards every story can meet. Business value ($10K) varies per story — a bug fix might deliver $500 in saved support costs. Customer feedback isn't feasible every sprint. If it varies by story, it's not DoD.

correctFeedback.text: DoD items must apply universally to EVERY story. Code review, test coverage, staging deployment, and AC verification are quality standards every story can meet. Business value ($10K) varies per story — a bug fix might deliver $500 in saved support costs. Customer feedback isn't feasible every sprint. If it varies by story, it's not DoD.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: If it varies by story, it's not DoD. DoD is the quality bar that makes ANY story production-ready — code reviewed, tested, deployed, verified. Universal standards only.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Ask yourself: can EVERY story meet this criterion? Code review — yes. $10K business value — no, some stories are small fixes. DoD must be universal.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Ask yourself: can EVERY story meet this criterion? Code review — yes. $10K business value — no, some stories are small fixes. DoD must be universal.

questions.retryHint: DoD items must apply to EVERY story universally. If an item depends on the specific story's size, type, or business context, it belongs elsewhere.

questions.question: Sort each criterion to the correct category — Acceptance Criteria or Definition of Done:
questions.situation: The team is working on Story US-120: "As a customer, I want to filter the menu by dietary restrictions." Alex needs the team to understand which criteria are story-specific (Acceptance Criteria) versus universal quality standards (Definition of Done).

chips.label: Filters for Veg, Vegan, GF, Dairy-Free available
Multiple filters can be selected simultaneously
Unit tests with 80% or greater coverage

dropZones.label: Acceptance Criteria
dropZones.detail: Story-specific: defines WHAT this particular story delivers

dropZones.label: Definition of Done
dropZones.detail: Universal: applies to ALL stories, not just this one

questions.explanation: Acceptance Criteria define WHAT this story delivers — filters work, results are fast, multi-select is supported. These change with every story. Definition of Done defines HOW we build quality — code reviewed, tested, deployed. These stay consistent across all stories. A story is only "done" when BOTH AC and DoD are met.

correctFeedback.text: Acceptance Criteria define WHAT this story delivers — filters work, results are fast, multi-select is supported. These change with every story. Definition of Done defines HOW we build quality — code reviewed, tested, deployed. These stay consistent across all stories. A story is only "done" when BOTH AC and DoD are met.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Got it! AC is what MY story delivers. DoD is the quality bar for ALL stories. I write the AC; the whole team agrees on the DoD.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Think about scope. Does this criterion apply only to the filter story, or to every story the team builds? Story-specific goes to AC. Universal goes to DoD.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Think about scope. Does this criterion apply only to the filter story, or to every story the team builds? Story-specific goes to AC. Universal goes to DoD.

questions.retryHint: AC = WHAT this specific story delivers (changes every story). DoD = HOW we ensure quality (stays the same for all stories).

questions.question: What should the team do?
questions.situation: Sprint 3 Review. The team committed to 8 stories. 4 stories meet both AC and DoD. The other 4 stories meet AC (features work) but NOT DoD — no code review, only 40% test coverage. Carlos asks: "Can we launch all 8 features to customers?"

options.text: Launch all 8 — acceptance criteria met means done
options.whyWrong: Shipping without code review and adequate testing puts untested code in front of customers. "It works" is not the same as "it's production-ready."

options.text: Launch only the 4 that meet DoD, fix the other 4 in Sprint 4
options.whyWrong: Partially correct — don't ship unfinished work — but the deeper issue is velocity. Those 4 stories shouldn't count as completed work for planning purposes.

options.text: Don't count the 4 incomplete stories as "done" — only credit velocity for stories meeting BOTH AC and DoD
Lower DoD standards so all 8 stories pass
options.whyWrong: Lowering standards to pass more stories defeats the purpose of DoD. It creates the illusion of progress while accumulating technical debt.

questions.explanation: Stories meeting AC but not DoD are "90% done" — they seem complete but aren't production-ready. Real velocity counts only stories meeting BOTH AC and DoD. That means velocity = 4 stories, not 8. Counting incomplete work inflates velocity, which makes future sprint planning unreliable and hides growing technical debt.

correctFeedback.text: Stories meeting AC but not DoD are "90% done" — they seem complete but aren't production-ready. Real velocity counts only stories meeting BOTH AC and DoD. That means velocity = 4 stories, not 8. Counting incomplete work inflates velocity, which makes future sprint planning unreliable and hides growing technical debt.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: 90% done is worthless to me. I need shippable increments, not 'almost done' features. Give me 4 real stories over 8 fake ones any day.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Would you serve a dish that tastes good but isn't plated, hasn't been checked for allergens, and hasn't left the kitchen? That's what shipping without DoD looks like.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Would you serve a dish that tastes good but isn't plated, hasn't been checked for allergens, and hasn't left the kitchen? That's what shipping without DoD looks like.

questions.retryHint: "Done" requires BOTH AC and DoD. If DoD isn't met, the story isn't done — regardless of whether features work. What does that mean for velocity?

questions.question: Sort each DoD item to the correct maturity phase:
questions.situation: Alex is planning how the team's DoD should evolve over their first 10 sprints. Some items are achievable immediately (Sprint 1), others require intermediate capability (Sprint 5), and advanced items need mature tooling (Sprint 10).

dropZones.label: Sprint 1 — Basic
dropZones.detail: Requires no special tooling; team can do this from day one

dropZones.label: Sprint 5 — Intermediate
dropZones.detail: Requires performance testing tools and baseline metrics to be established

dropZones.label: Sprint 8-10 — Advanced
dropZones.detail: Requires mature CI/CD infrastructure and automation expertise

questions.explanation: DoD evolves as team capability grows. Sprint 1: basic items requiring no special tooling (review, tests). Sprint 5: intermediate items needing established tools (performance testing). Sprint 8-10: advanced items requiring mature infrastructure (automation, production deployment, monitoring). Don't start with a 20-item DoD if you lack the tools to achieve it.

correctFeedback.text: DoD evolves as team capability grows. Sprint 1: basic items requiring no special tooling (review, tests). Sprint 5: intermediate items needing established tools (performance testing). Sprint 8-10: advanced items requiring mature infrastructure (automation, production deployment, monitoring). Don't start with a 20-item DoD if you lack the tools to achieve it.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Start with what the team can achieve TODAY. Build capability, then expand DoD. A 3-item DoD you actually follow beats a 15-item DoD you ignore.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Think about what tools and skills are needed. Can a brand-new team deploy to production automatically on day one? Match DoD to capability.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Think about what tools and skills are needed. Can a brand-new team deploy to production automatically on day one? Match DoD to capability.

questions.retryHint: Match DoD items to capability. What can the team do from day one with no special tools? What requires infrastructure that takes months to build?

questions.question: How does Definition of Done ensure "incrementally releasable product increments"?
questions.situation: Without DoD, a team ships features that "work" but have bugs lurking, aren't properly tested, and crash in production. The Product Owner wants to know how DoD changes this outcome.

options.text: DoD speeds up development by reducing the number of requirements
options.whyWrong: DoD doesn't reduce requirements — it adds quality gates. Development may take slightly longer per story, but the product is always shippable.

options.text: DoD ensures every completed story meets quality standards, making the product potentially shippable after each sprint
DoD eliminates the need for acceptance testing by the Product Owner
options.whyWrong: DoD complements AC, not replaces it. The PO still verifies acceptance criteria. DoD adds technical quality checks on top of functional acceptance.

options.text: DoD replaces the Product Owner's role in accepting work
options.whyWrong: The PO's role is unchanged. DoD adds team-level quality standards alongside the PO's story-level acceptance.

questions.explanation: DoD is a quality gate. Every story meeting DoD is production-ready — code reviewed, tested, deployed to staging. That means the product increment after Sprint 1 can ship. Sprint 2 can ship. The business decides WHEN to release, but the option is always there because quality is built in, not added later.

correctFeedback.text: DoD is a quality gate. Every story meeting DoD is production-ready — code reviewed, tested, deployed to staging. That means the product increment after Sprint 1 can ship. Sprint 2 can ship. The business decides WHEN to release, but the option is always there because quality is built in, not added later.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: With DoD, I know that when I accept a story, it's not just feature-complete — it's production-ready. I can confidently tell Carlos we CAN ship after any sprint.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: DoD doesn't reduce work or replace the PO. It ensures that completed work meets a quality bar so the product is always in a potentially shippable state.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: DoD doesn't reduce work or replace the PO. It ensures that completed work meets a quality bar so the product is always in a potentially shippable state.

questions.retryHint: "Incrementally releasable" means the product COULD ship after any sprint. What must be true about every completed story for that to work?

tabs.title: What Is Definition of Done?
tabs.content:
A **shared team agreement** specifying quality standards that EVERY user story must meet to be considered "done." It's the quality gate between "feature works" and "product is shippable."

**Purpose:**
1. **Prevents "90% done" syndrome** — features work but aren't production-ready
2. **Ensures incrementally releasable product** — business can ship after any sprint
3. **Creates shared understanding** — eliminates "my done vs your done" confusion
4. **Builds quality in from day 1** — prevents technical debt accumulation

**Sample DoD (Sprint 1):**

| # | DoD Item |
|---|----------|
| 1 | Code reviewed by at least 1 developer |
| 2 | Unit tests with 80% or greater coverage |
| 3 | Acceptance criteria verified by PO |
| 4 | Deployed to staging environment |
| 5 | No critical bugs (severity 1-2) |
| 6 | Code merged to main branch |
| 7 | Documentation updated |

tabs.title: DoD vs Acceptance Criteria
tabs.content:
Two different things. Both required. A story is only "done" when BOTH are met.

| Aspect | Acceptance Criteria | Definition of Done |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Scope** | Story-specific | All stories |
| **Defines** | WHAT this story delivers | HOW we build quality |
| **Written by** | Product Owner | Entire team |
| **Changes** | Every story different | Consistent (evolves slowly) |
| **Example** | "Filter by diet works" | "Code reviewed by peer" |

**The Done Equation:**

**Story DONE = Acceptance Criteria Met + Definition of Done Met**

- Without DoD: Features work but aren't shippable (90% done syndrome)
- With DoD: Every sprint delivers a potentially releasable product increment

tabs.title: DoD Evolution Over Time
tabs.content:
DoD grows as team capability matures. Don't start with 20 items if you lack the tools.

| Phase | Sprint | DoD Items |
|-------|--------|-----------|
| **Basic** | 1-3 | Code review, unit tests, staging deploy, no critical bugs |
| **Intermediate** | 5-7 | + Performance testing, security scan, accessibility check |
| **Advanced** | 8-10 | + CI/CD automation, production deploy, monitoring/alerts |

**What Triggers Evolution:**
- **New tools available** — CI/CD pipeline built, so add automation requirement
- **Production issues** — outage occurred, so add monitoring requirement
- **Team consistently exceeds threshold** — hitting 90% coverage, so raise the bar from 80%

**Key Principle:** DoD should stretch the team slightly beyond comfort — not set impossible standards and not stay so easy it's meaningless.

diagram.alt: DoD evolution from basic to advanced

tabs.title: Who Owns the DoD?
tabs.content:
**The entire Scrum team** creates and owns the DoD collaboratively:
- **Developers** contribute technical quality items (code review, test coverage, deployment)
- **Product Owner** contributes acceptance and quality items (AC verified, no critical bugs)
- **Scrum Master** facilitates creation and ensures the team follows it

**Common Misconception:** "The PO defines DoD like they define AC."

**Reality:** Unlike Acceptance Criteria (PO-authored, story-specific), DoD is a team agreement. Every team member has input because it covers technical, functional, and operational quality.

content.title: DoD for a Brand New Team

scenario.title: DoD for a Brand New Team
scenario.description: A NEW team is launching to build a catering management system for Savory & Co.'s corporate events division. They have basic CI/CD (staging only, no production pipeline), no automated testing infrastructure yet, and 4 developers (2 senior, 2 junior). You're facilitating their Sprint 0 DoD workshop.

images.alt: New team creating their first DoD

questions.question: Which items are appropriate for this new team's Sprint 1 DoD? Select all that apply.
questions.scenario_name: Appropriate DoD Items for a New Team
questions.situation: The new catering team is creating their first DoD in Sprint 0. They have staging CI/CD but no production pipeline, and no automated testing infrastructure. You need to help them select achievable DoD items that match their current capability.

options.text: Code reviewed by a senior developer
Manual testing completed and signed off
Acceptance criteria verified by Product Owner
Deployed to staging environment
Automated tests with 90% or greater coverage
options.whyWrong: The team has no automated testing infrastructure. Requiring 90% automated coverage when the tooling doesn't exist guarantees failure and frustration.

options.text: Deployed to production with zero-downtime release
options.whyWrong: The team has staging CI/CD only — no production pipeline. Requiring production deployment when the capability doesn't exist makes the DoD unachievable.

questions.explanation: Start with DoD items matching team capability. The team can do code reviews (senior devs available), manual testing (no automation needed), AC verification (PO is present), and staging deployment (CI/CD exists for staging). Automated 90% coverage is impossible without automation infrastructure. Production deployment is impossible without a production pipeline. Add these later as capability matures.

correctFeedback.text: Start with DoD items matching team capability. The team can do code reviews (senior devs available), manual testing (no automation needed), AC verification (PO is present), and staging deployment (CI/CD exists for staging). Automated 90% coverage is impossible without automation infrastructure. Production deployment is impossible without a production pipeline. Add these later as capability matures.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Start with what you CAN do today. Review, manual test, verify, deploy to staging. When you build automation and a production pipeline, expand the DoD to match.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Look at what tools and infrastructure this team actually has. Can they run automated tests today? Can they deploy to production? If not, those items don't belong in Sprint 1 DoD.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Look at what tools and infrastructure this team actually has. Can they run automated tests today? Can they deploy to production? If not, those items don't belong in Sprint 1 DoD.

questions.retryHint: Match DoD to capability. What can this team actually do with their current tools and skills? If they don't have the infrastructure, they can't require it.

questions.question: Why shouldn't this new team start with the advanced DoD?
questions.scenario_name: Why Not Start with Advanced DoD?
questions.situation: Devon suggests: "Why don't we start with the advanced DoD — automated tests, production deployment, monitoring? That way we're building quality from day one." The senior developers look skeptical.

options.text: Advanced DoD is always wrong for any team
options.whyWrong: Advanced DoD is excellent for mature teams with established infrastructure. It's wrong for THIS team because they lack the tools, not because it's inherently bad.

options.text: The team lacks the tools and processes to achieve it — start basic, evolve as capability grows
New teams should skip DoD entirely for their first 3 sprints
options.whyWrong: Skipping DoD means 3 sprints of undefined quality — exactly what causes the "90% done" problem. Even a basic 4-item DoD is better than none.

options.text: DoD should only ever contain exactly 3 items
options.whyWrong: DoD length depends on team maturity. Some teams have 5 items, others have 15. The number isn't the point — achievability is.

questions.explanation: DoD must match capability. No CI/CD for production? You can't require production deployment. No automation infrastructure? You can't require automated tests. Setting unachievable standards leads to either an ignored DoD (team gives up) or a frustrated team (constant failure). Start with achievable items, build capability, then expand.

correctFeedback.text: DoD must match capability. No CI/CD for production? You can't require production deployment. No automation infrastructure? You can't require automated tests. Setting unachievable standards leads to either an ignored DoD (team gives up) or a frustrated team (constant failure). Start with achievable items, build capability, then expand.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Oh, I see. It's not about wanting less quality — it's about being honest about what we can achieve NOW and building toward more. Start basic, grow into advanced.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: DoD isn't one-size-fits-all. A mature team with full CI/CD should have an advanced DoD. A new team without those tools needs a DoD that matches reality, then evolves.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: DoD isn't one-size-fits-all. A mature team with full CI/CD should have an advanced DoD. A new team without those tools needs a DoD that matches reality, then evolves.

questions.retryHint: The issue isn't the quality aspiration — it's whether the team has the tools to achieve it. What happens when you require something the team can't deliver?

questions.question: When should this team add "Automated tests with 90% coverage" to their DoD?
questions.situation: Three sprints in, the team has built a CI/CD automation pipeline that runs reliably. Devon asks: "Should we update our DoD now that we have automation?"

options.text: Sprint 1 — push the team to learn automation fast
options.whyWrong: In Sprint 1, the automation pipeline doesn't exist yet. Requiring automated tests before the infrastructure is built is setting the team up to fail.

options.text: When the CI/CD automation pipeline is built and proven stable — likely around Sprint 5-8
Never — automation is optional and shouldn't be in DoD
options.whyWrong: Automation is a significant quality improvement. Once the capability exists, not including it in DoD means the team won't consistently use it.

options.text: Only when a manager demands it as a policy requirement
options.whyWrong: DoD is a team agreement, not a management mandate. The team evolves DoD based on capability growth, not top-down directives.

questions.explanation: Add DoD items when capability exists. The team built a CI/CD pipeline and proved it works reliably — now they can require automated tests in their DoD. Evolution is triggered by capability growth, not management mandates or arbitrary timelines. The team decides when they're ready.

correctFeedback.text: Add DoD items when capability exists. The team built a CI/CD pipeline and proved it works reliably — now they can require automated tests in their DoD. Evolution is triggered by capability growth, not management mandates or arbitrary timelines. The team decides when they're ready.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: DoD evolution follows capability. Build the tool, prove it works, THEN add it to DoD. Don't require what the team can't deliver — and don't hold back when they can.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: The pipeline is built and stable. The team has the capability now. Why would you wait for a manager to tell you to use it? DoD evolution is the team's decision.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: The pipeline is built and stable. The team has the capability now. Why would you wait for a manager to tell you to use it? DoD evolution is the team's decision.

questions.retryHint: What triggers DoD evolution? New capability. The team built automation — now they can require it. Who decides? The team, not management.

content.title: PMP Practice Questions
content.description: Test your knowledge with real PMP-style questions

questions.question: According to Scrum principles, what is the status of this story?
questions.scenario_name: AC Met but DoD Not Met
questions.situation: A Scrum team completes a user story that meets all acceptance criteria. The feature works correctly in the developer's local environment. However, the code has not been peer-reviewed and test coverage is only 30%, while the team's DoD requires code review and 80% test coverage.

options.text: Done — acceptance criteria are met and the feature works
options.whyWrong: AC met alone does not equal done. DoD exists specifically to catch this gap — features that work but aren't production-ready.

options.text: Not done — the Definition of Done has not been met
Partially done — count it as half a story point for velocity
options.whyWrong: There is no "partially done" in Scrum. A story either meets DoD or it doesn't. Partial credit inflates velocity and hides incomplete work.

options.text: Done — code review and test coverage are optional quality extras
options.whyWrong: Code review and test coverage are not optional if they're in the DoD. The team agreed these are required quality standards for every story.

questions.explanation: A story is only "done" when BOTH Acceptance Criteria AND Definition of Done are met. The feature works (AC met), but code review and test coverage requirements are not satisfied (DoD not met). The story is NOT done, should NOT count toward velocity, and must be completed in a future sprint.

correctFeedback.text: A story is only "done" when BOTH Acceptance Criteria AND Definition of Done are met. The feature works (AC met), but code review and test coverage requirements are not satisfied (DoD not met). The story is NOT done, should NOT count toward velocity, and must be completed in a future sprint.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: Working code is necessary but not sufficient. Without code review and adequate tests, this story is a ticking time bomb. It's not done until DoD says it's done.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Remember: Story DONE = AC Met + DoD Met. If either piece is missing, the story isn't done. Would you serve a dish that's cooked but not plated or checked for allergens?
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Remember: Story DONE = AC Met + DoD Met. If either piece is missing, the story isn't done. Would you serve a dish that's cooked but not plated or checked for allergens?

questions.retryHint: What two things must BOTH be true for a story to be "done"? If either is missing, what's the status?

questions.question: What is the PRIMARY difference between Acceptance Criteria and Definition of Done?
questions.situation: A junior team member asks: "What's the difference between Acceptance Criteria and Definition of Done? They both seem like checklists that a story needs to pass." The Scrum Master needs to explain the primary distinction clearly.

options.text: Acceptance Criteria are written by developers; DoD is written by the Product Owner
options.whyWrong: The authorship is reversed. The PO writes Acceptance Criteria (story-specific). The entire team creates DoD (universal quality). Developers don't write AC.

options.text: Acceptance Criteria define story-specific success conditions; DoD defines team-level quality standards for all stories
Acceptance Criteria are optional; Definition of Done is mandatory
options.whyWrong: Both are important. AC isn't optional — it defines what the story must deliver. Neither is truly "optional" in a well-run Scrum team.

options.text: They are the same thing with different names used in different contexts
options.whyWrong: They serve fundamentally different purposes. AC = story scope. DoD = quality standards. Conflating them leads to the "90% done" problem.

questions.explanation: Acceptance Criteria are unique to each story — they define WHAT the story delivers (e.g., "filter by diet works"). Definition of Done applies to EVERY story — it defines HOW we ensure quality (e.g., "code reviewed, tests pass, deployed to staging"). AC changes per story; DoD stays consistent. Both must be met for a story to be done.

correctFeedback.text: Acceptance Criteria are unique to each story — they define WHAT the story delivers (e.g., "filter by diet works"). Definition of Done applies to EVERY story — it defines HOW we ensure quality (e.g., "code reviewed, tests pass, deployed to staging"). AC changes per story; DoD stays consistent. Both must be met for a story to be done.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: I write the Acceptance Criteria for each story — what it needs to deliver. The whole team agrees on DoD — the quality bar every story must clear. Different scope, different authors, both required.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: Think about what changes and what stays the same. Does this criterion apply only to one story, or to every story the team builds? That's the key distinction.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: Think about what changes and what stays the same. Does this criterion apply only to one story, or to every story the team builds? That's the key distinction.

questions.retryHint: One changes every story, one stays consistent. One defines WHAT, the other defines HOW. Which is which?

questions.question: What should the Scrum Master recommend?
questions.situation: A team's Definition of Done includes "automated tests with 90% coverage," but they recently adopted CI/CD and can only achieve 60% coverage reliably. Most stories fail the DoD check, causing frustration. The Scrum Master is asked to recommend an approach.

options.text: Remove automated testing from DoD entirely since the team struggles with it
options.whyWrong: Removing testing entirely is overcorrecting. The team CAN do 60% — that's meaningful quality. Removing it eliminates the quality gate rather than right-sizing it.

options.text: Keep the 90% requirement and accept that most stories won't meet DoD
options.whyWrong: Consistent DoD failures demoralize the team and make DoD meaningless. If stories routinely fail, the team stops taking DoD seriously.

options.text: Adjust DoD to match current capability (60%), with a plan to evolve toward 90% as tooling matures
Assign responsibility to specific developers who can't reach 90% coverage
options.whyWrong: Blaming individuals undermines team collaboration. DoD is a team agreement, and capability gaps are solved with tooling and training, not blame.

questions.explanation: DoD should match team capability. Setting unachievable standards (90% when tooling only supports 60%) leads to either an ignored DoD or a demoralized team. Adjust to achievable standards (60%), then evolve upward as tooling matures and the team gains expertise. The goal is continuous improvement, not perfection from day one.

correctFeedback.text: DoD should match team capability. Setting unachievable standards (90% when tooling only supports 60%) leads to either an ignored DoD or a demoralized team. Adjust to achievable standards (60%), then evolve upward as tooling matures and the team gains expertise. The goal is continuous improvement, not perfection from day one.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: DoD should stretch the team slightly — not break them. Set it at 60% today, build toward 75%, then 90%. Continuous improvement beats impossible standards.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: An ignored DoD is worse than no DoD. If the bar is unreachable, the team stops trying. Match DoD to reality, then raise the bar as capability grows.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: An ignored DoD is worse than no DoD. If the bar is unreachable, the team stops trying. Match DoD to reality, then raise the bar as capability grows.

questions.retryHint: What happens when DoD is unachievable? The team either ignores it or gets frustrated. What's the better approach — remove it, keep failing, or adjust to reality?

questions.question: Why does Definition of Done ensure "incrementally releasable product increments"?
questions.situation: A stakeholder questions the value of DoD: "We spend extra time meeting these quality checks every sprint. Why not just test everything at the end before the big release?" The PM needs to explain why DoD enables incremental releasability.

options.text: DoD reduces the total amount of work needed per sprint
options.whyWrong: DoD doesn't reduce work — it adds quality checks. Stories may take slightly longer, but the product is always releasable, eliminating costly end-of-project testing.

options.text: DoD ensures every completed story meets quality standards, making the product potentially shippable after each sprint
DoD eliminates the need for end-of-project integration testing
options.whyWrong: DoD reduces but doesn't eliminate integration testing needs. Complex system interactions may still require additional testing beyond story-level DoD.

options.text: DoD allows the team to skip user acceptance testing
options.whyWrong: User acceptance testing (UAT) and DoD serve different purposes. DoD ensures technical quality; UAT validates business value. Both may still be needed.

questions.explanation: When every story meets DoD — reviewed, tested, deployed to staging — the product increment is production-ready after each sprint. The business can release after Sprint 1, Sprint 2, or any sprint. "Test everything at the end" means the product isn't shippable until the final sprint, removing the agile advantage of incremental delivery.

correctFeedback.text: When every story meets DoD — reviewed, tested, deployed to staging — the product increment is production-ready after each sprint. The business can release after Sprint 1, Sprint 2, or any sprint. "Test everything at the end" means the product isn't shippable until the final sprint, removing the agile advantage of incremental delivery.
correctFeedback.characterQuote: With DoD, I know I CAN launch after any sprint if the business needs it. Without DoD, I have to wait until everything is tested at the end — which defeats the whole point of agile.

incorrectFeedback.fallbackText: DoD doesn't reduce work or skip testing. It ensures quality is built into every story so the product is always in a shippable state. That's the agile advantage.
incorrectFeedback.characterQuote: DoD doesn't reduce work or skip testing. It ensures quality is built into every story so the product is always in a shippable state. That's the agile advantage.

questions.retryHint: "Incrementally releasable" means you COULD ship after any sprint. What must be true about every completed story for that to be possible?

content.title: Lesson Complete!
content.summary: You've learned that the **Definition of Done** is the team's quality gate — a shared checklist ensuring every story is production-ready, not just "feature complete." DoD differs from Acceptance Criteria, evolves with team capability, and prevents the costly "90% done" syndrome.
key_takeaways: DoD is a shared team agreement — universal quality standards that EVERY story must meet (code review, tests, deployment, verification)
Story DONE = AC Met + DoD Met — Acceptance Criteria define WHAT the story delivers; DoD defines HOW we ensure quality. Both are required.
DoD prevents "90% done" syndrome — features that work but aren't production-ready don't count as done and shouldn't inflate velocity
DoD evolves with capability — start basic (review, manual test, staging), add intermediate (performance), advance to mature (automation, production, monitoring)
The whole team owns DoD — developers add technical items, PO adds acceptance items, Scrum Master facilitates. It's a team agreement, not a management mandate.
"Incrementally releasable" requires DoD — when every completed story meets DoD, the product can potentially ship after any sprint

character_closing.name: Alex Mitchell
character_closing.quote: I used to think 'done' was obvious — features work, ship them. Then I saw what happens without a quality gate: code that crashes in production, velocity numbers that lie, and technical debt that compounds every sprint. The Definition of Done isn't extra work — it's the agreement that makes ALL our work actually count. Start basic, evolve as you grow, and never let 'it works on my machine' pass for done.
